sections.title: Why an Engineer Needs This Chapter
lines: Chapter one, section one. Why an engineer needs this chapter.

Synthetic biology sits at the meeting point of two cultures: the wet-lab molecular biologist who has been thinking about cells since their undergraduate years, and the engineer or physical scientist who has decided that cells are the most interesting programmable substrate they have ever encountered.
This first chapter of the primer is written with that second group squarely in mind.
The authors are not trying to turn you into a molecular biologist; they are trying to give you the smallest possible foundation that will let you do two specific things.
The first is to model biological systems intelligently, in the same sense that you might model a circuit or a control loop.
The second, just as important, is to communicate with biologists without constantly tripping over vocabulary or asking what feels, to them, like incredibly basic questions.
If you can do both of those things, you can participate in synthetic biology as a designer rather than as a tourist.
The chapter is deliberately compact, and you should treat it as a survey rather than a deep textbook.
If a particular topic snags your interest, the authors are explicit that you should chase it down in a dedicated molecular biology textbook.
Their goal here is breadth, not depth.
The chapter pivots around two ideas that come back over and over in everything that follows.
Idea one: how information flows in biological systems.
Idea two: how that flow of information is controlled.

Almost every engineering choice you will make in synthetic biology is, in some form, an intervention on one of those two layers — either changing what information is being stored or transmitted, or changing the conditions under which it is read out.
If you can keep those two layers cleanly separated in your head, you will find that a lot of the messiness of biology resolves into a structure you can reason about.
Before we get into the mechanisms, though, it is worth pausing on what makes biology genuinely different from other engineering disciplines, because those differences are not just academic.
They reshape what "designing a system" even means.
The first difference is physical layout.
Biology is not programmed onto a printed circuit board.
Interactions inside a cell are not defined by where wires run; they are defined by which molecules happen to meet each other in the crowded soup that fills the cell.
Two proteins interact because their shapes are complementary and their concentrations are high enough that they bump into each other often enough — not because someone soldered a trace between them.
That changes everything about modularity and connection.
The second difference is natural selection.
Cells are subject to evolution.

Any modification you introduce that is deleterious to the cell — slower growth, wasted energy, toxic by-products — will be selected against, and the population you carefully engineered will get outcompeted by spontaneous mutants that have undone your work.
Engineers building aircraft do not have this problem.
Aircraft do not mutate.
Biology demands a different definition of robustness, one that includes evolutionary stability, not just mechanical reliability.
And third, biological systems exhibit emergent behaviour and complexity at a scale that engineers sometimes underestimate.

These concepts may already feel familiar from non-biological systems, but inside a living cell they can show up in ways that are surprising, that propagate across pathways you did not realise were connected, and that make a clean, predictive design difficult.
Keep these three differences in mind throughout the chapter.

When the authors describe a mechanism, ask yourself: how would natural selection erode my design here? Could I get an unwanted emergent behaviour from this interaction? What molecular collision am I actually relying on?
Those questions are how an engineer learns to think like a synthetic biologist, and they are exactly the questions the rest of this book will help you answer.

sections.title: DNA Structure
lines: Section two. DNA structure.
DNA — deoxyribonucleic acid — is the molecule that stores hereditary information in essentially every living organism.

Its discovery, and the recognition that the double helix is its structural form, is one of those rare moments in science whose iconography has escaped from the laboratory and entered the wider culture.
But to design with DNA, you need to understand its structure in considerably more detail than that famous spiral suggests.
DNA is a polymer.
It is built by linking together repeating subunits called nucleotides, in much the same way that any polymer is built by linking monomers.
Each nucleotide has three parts.
First, a phosphate group — a phosphorus atom surrounded by oxygens, carrying a negative charge at physiological pH.
Second, a five-carbon sugar called deoxyribose — the "deoxy" prefix refers to the fact that one position on the sugar is missing the hydroxyl group that ribose has.
Third, a nitrogenous base — and this is the part that varies.
There are four possible bases in DNA, and these are the famous letters of the genetic alphabet: adenine, guanine, cytosine, and thymine, abbreviated A, G, C, and T.
The phosphate and the sugar are the same in every nucleotide, regardless of which base is attached.
They link to each other to form the backbone of a DNA strand — a long, repeating, slightly negatively charged scaffold.
The bases hang off this backbone like beads on a string, and it is the order of the bases — the sequence — that constitutes information.
So when biologists write down a DNA sequence as a long string of A's, G's, C's, and T's, they are writing down the order of those bases along the backbone.
A single DNA strand, on its own, is already a chain of information.
But DNA in cells is almost always double-stranded — two of these chains running side by side, paired up.
And the way the pairing works is the key to almost everything DNA does.
Each of the four bases can only pair with one specific partner.
A pairs with T, and G pairs with C.
Those are the only valid pairings.
A cannot pair with G; G cannot pair with T; and so on.
This rule is called complementarity, and it is not arbitrary — it is a consequence of the chemistry of the bases.
The bases form hydrogen bonds between specific donor and acceptor atoms on each base.
A donor is a position that has a hydrogen atom available to donate to a hydrogen bond; an acceptor is a position with a lone pair of electrons ready to receive one.

When you look at the geometry of A and T, the hydrogen bond donors and acceptors line up perfectly across the gap between them — two hydrogen bonds form, with A's donor across from T's acceptor and vice versa.
The same is true for G and C, except that G and C share three hydrogen bonds instead of two.
Try to pair, say, A with G, and the donors and acceptors do not line up; you would be asking two donors to bond to each other, or two acceptors, and the chemistry forbids it.
That is why complementarity is exclusive.
Because G–C pairs share three hydrogen bonds and A–T pairs share only two, a stretch of DNA that is rich in G's and C's is more stable, and harder to pull apart, than a stretch rich in A's and T's.
This matters enormously in the lab: it is why PCR primers are designed with care to their GC content, and why melting temperatures of DNA depend so strongly on sequence.
Complementarity also gives biology a free gift, which is redundancy.
Every base on one strand has a partner base on the other strand that uniquely specifies what it should be.
So a cell carrying double-stranded DNA is, in effect, carrying two copies of the same information.
If one strand gets damaged, the other strand can be used as a template to repair it.
Most DNA repair pathways exploit exactly this property.
Beyond pairing, there is another structural feature to mention: stacking.
The bases are flat, aromatic molecules — they are planar.

When two complementary strands wind around each other, the bases stack on top of one another like a pile of plates, and this stacking is energetically favourable because it hides the bases from water.
At the same time, the phosphate backbone is negatively charged, and the phosphates repel each other.
The sugar that links phosphate to base provides a fixed-length spacer.

Balance all of that — stacking pulling the bases together, phosphate repulsion pushing the backbones apart, sugar geometry constraining the distance — and you do not get two parallel ladders; you get a twist.
Each successive base pair is rotated by roughly 34 degrees relative to the one below it.
Over the course of about ten base pairs, the twist completes a full turn.
That is where the famous double helix comes from: it is the geometric consequence of complementary pairing plus base stacking plus charge repulsion.
Hold this picture in your mind as we go forward.

DNA is not just a sequence of letters; it is a physical, twisted, charged, hydrogen-bonded molecule, and the engineering choices you make in synthetic biology will be felt by every one of those physical properties.

sections.title: DNA Replication
lines: Section three. DNA replication.
One of the defining features of life is the ability to make a copy of oneself.
At the molecular level, that ability boils down to the ability to copy DNA.
Replication is the process by which a single double-stranded DNA molecule becomes two identical double-stranded molecules, each of which can then be passed to a daughter cell.
The complementarity we just discussed is the key insight that makes this possible.

Because the sequence of one strand uniquely determines the sequence of the other, if you separate the two strands of a DNA double helix, you can use each one as a template to synthesise a brand new complementary strand.
Do that to both strands simultaneously and you have produced two daughter helices.
Each daughter helix contains one strand from the original parent molecule and one freshly synthesised strand.
Biologists call this "semi-conservative" replication, because the parental information is conserved into both daughters in this half-and-half pattern.
The cell does not perform this magic on its own.
DNA replication is carried out by a family of enzymes called DNA polymerases.
A DNA polymerase reads along a template strand, base by base, and builds the new complementary strand by selecting and incorporating the correct nucleotides one at a time.
The building blocks it uses are not the bare nucleotides we described before; they are nucleotides that arrive at the polymerase already charged up with energy.

Specifically, the polymerase takes deoxyribonucleotide triphosphates — abbreviated dNTPs — and uses the energy of the triphosphate group to drive the formation of the new phosphate bond joining the incoming nucleotide to the growing strand.
The triphosphate is sliced off, releasing the energy of two of its phosphate bonds, and a pyrophosphate is released as a by-product.

This pattern — using a triphosphate as a high-energy substrate — is something you will see again and again in cellular biochemistry; the most famous example is ATP, the near-universal energy currency of cells.
How does the polymerase pick the right nucleotide to add?
It does not have to think very hard, because it is constrained by base pairing.
Only the nucleotide whose base is complementary to the next base on the template can form the correct hydrogen bond pattern in the polymerase's active site.
The polymerase tests incoming nucleotides, accepts the one that fits, and rejects the rest.
Polymerases also have a couple of structural quirks that have major consequences for how replication actually happens in practice.
The first is polarity.
DNA strands have direction, because the deoxyribose sugar is asymmetric — one end of a strand terminates with a 5-prime carbon of the sugar, the other end with a 3-prime carbon.
These are conventionally called the 5-prime and 3-prime ends.
When the two strands of a double helix come together, they always run anti-parallel — the 5-prime end of one strand sits opposite the 3-prime end of the other.
DNA polymerases can only add new nucleotides to the 3-prime end of an existing strand.
They cannot add at the 5-prime end.
The second quirk is that polymerases cannot start from nothing.
They can extend an existing chain — but they cannot just look at a bare single-stranded template and decide to begin synthesising.
They need a starting handle: a short stretch of already-double-stranded nucleic acid with a free 3-prime end that the polymerase can grab onto and elongate.
That short starting handle is called a primer.
In a living cell, the primer is supplied by a different enzyme called primase, which lays down a short RNA primer right where DNA replication needs to begin.
DNA polymerase then takes over and extends the primer using DNA nucleotides.
The RNA primer is later removed and replaced with DNA — there is a whole separate machinery for that.
In the laboratory, we exploit the same fundamental requirement, but we use it for our own purposes.
Instead of letting the cell decide where to prime, we synthesise short DNA oligonucleotides — primers — and choose exactly where they should bind on a template.
Because polymerases only extend from the 3-prime end of a primer, the choice of primer sequence dictates exactly where the polymerase begins synthesising and which direction it goes.
This turns out to be one of the most powerful design controls in all of molecular biology — and it is what makes the next section, the polymerase chain reaction, possible.

So as you internalise replication, fix these properties in your head: it is template-directed, semi-conservative, energy-driven by triphosphates, direction-dependent on the 3-prime end of the new strand, and absolutely dependent on a primer to get started.
Almost every DNA-handling technique in modern biology is, in some sense, a clever use of those constraints.

sections.title: PCR — The Polymerase Chain Reaction
lines: Section four. The polymerase chain reaction, or PCR.
If you only learn one wet-lab technique in molecular biology, learn this one.

PCR is the single most important and most widely used DNA technique of the last forty years, and the reason it is worth dwelling on is that to understand it, you have to weave together everything we just covered: complementary base pairing, polymerase polarity, the need for primers, and the requirement for an energy source.
If you can explain PCR clearly to someone, you have probably understood the basics of DNA.
What does PCR do?
It amplifies DNA — exponentially.
You hand the reaction a tiny, vanishingly small amount of a specific DNA template, and it hands you back, a couple of hours later, billions of copies of one particular stretch of that template.
Two things make PCR powerful.
The first is speed — the doubling time of the reaction is about a minute per cycle.
The second is specificity — out of an entire genome of DNA, you can amplify just one particular gene or fragment, dictated entirely by the primers you design.
Let me walk through how the trick works.
First ingredient: a thermostable DNA polymerase.
Why thermostable?
Because the reaction is going to swing the temperature up to 95 degrees Celsius repeatedly, and an ordinary polymerase would denature and be useless after the first cycle.

The breakthrough was the discovery of polymerases isolated from extremophile organisms — bacteria that live in places like hot springs and hydrothermal vents, where ambient temperatures routinely exceed 100 degrees Celsius.
The classic example is Taq polymerase, from Thermus aquaticus, which can shrug off the temperature cycling of PCR.
Second ingredient: two primers — short, synthetic, single-stranded DNA oligonucleotides, typically twenty to thirty nucleotides long.
One primer is complementary to one end of the region you want to amplify, on one strand.
The other primer is complementary to the other end of that region, on the opposite strand.
Their job is to bracket your target.
Third ingredient: dNTPs, the energy-loaded nucleotide building blocks the polymerase will use to extend the primers.
Fourth ingredient: a buffer with the right salts and pH to keep everything happy.
Now the temperature cycling.
Step one is denaturation.
You heat the reaction to about 95 degrees Celsius.
All the double-stranded DNA in the tube melts apart into single strands — the hydrogen bonds between base pairs are not strong enough to survive that temperature.
Step two is annealing.
You cool the reaction down — typically to somewhere between 50 and 65 degrees Celsius, the exact temperature being tuned to the melting temperature of your specific primers.
At this temperature, single strands of DNA are looking for partners to base-pair with.

Because the primers are in massive excess compared to the template, the primers find their complementary sequences on the template strands much faster than the long template strands can find each other.
So the primers anneal to their targets.
Step three is extension.
You raise the temperature to about 72 degrees Celsius, which is the optimal temperature for Taq and similar thermostable polymerases.
The polymerase grabs each primer at its 3-prime end and starts adding dNTPs, synthesising a new complementary strand off the template.
By the end of the extension step, you have doubled the amount of the bracketed region.
Step four: repeat.
Heat back to 95 to melt the new double-stranded products, cool to anneal again, warm to extend again.
Each cycle doubles the amount of the targeted region.
After about 25 to 30 cycles of doubling, your one initial copy has become roughly one billion copies.
The exponential nature is everything.
You can see why the original Taq polymerase — although it works — was not perfect.
Its fidelity is relatively low, which means it occasionally inserts the wrong nucleotide during extension, and over many cycles those mistakes accumulate in the amplified products.
For many uses — diagnostics, for example, or rough cloning — this is acceptable.
For uses where you want a pristine, sequence-accurate copy, biologists have isolated and engineered better polymerases.

The key feature added is a proofreading domain — a 3-prime to 5-prime exonuclease activity, which means that after the polymerase incorporates a nucleotide, a separate part of the same enzyme can check the pairing and chew back any mismatched base before continuing.
Proofreading polymerases dramatically improve PCR accuracy.
Another engineering target has been processivity — the average distance the polymerase travels before falling off the DNA.

Engineered polymerases like Phusion have an added DNA-binding domain that grips the substrate more tightly, which means PCR can amplify much longer fragments than the original Taq enzyme could manage.

Modern high-fidelity, high-processivity polymerases can amplify whole plasmid backbones — many kilobases at a time — opening the door to cloning techniques like Q5 site-directed mutagenesis and around-the-horn PCR that were impractical before.
PCR is the foundation under nearly every workflow we will encounter later in this book: cloning, sequencing, diagnostics, genotyping, library construction.
Whenever you read about a synthetic biology technique that needs a specific piece of DNA, the implicit first step is almost always: amplify it by PCR.

sections.title: Information Flow and the Central Dogma
lines: Section five. Information flow in biology, and what biologists call the central dogma.
We have established that DNA is the molecule that stores information.
But cells are not just sequences of bases sitting in a library.
Cells move, divide, sense, respond, produce things — they have function.
Most of that function is carried out by proteins.
So we need to ask a very basic question: how does information stored in DNA become functional protein?
The conceptual answer is what is known as the central dogma of molecular biology.
The central dogma states that information flows from DNA, to RNA, to protein.
DNA is transcribed into messenger RNA, abbreviated mRNA.
And messenger RNA is then translated into protein.
Those two steps — transcription and translation — are the two great machines of gene expression, and you will spend a lot of your synthetic-biology life trying to control one or both of them.
Before we look at the machinery, let us pin down a vocabulary item: the gene.
A gene is the fundamental unit of hereditary information.
In most cases, a gene is a stretch of DNA whose sequence encodes a protein.
But not always — some genes do not code for proteins at all, and instead the RNA molecule that gets made from them is itself the functional product.
Ribosomal RNA, transfer RNA, microRNA — these are functional RNAs and they will come up later.
Importantly, when biologists talk about a gene, they do not just mean the coding sequence in isolation.
They mean the coding sequence plus the regulatory elements that control when, where, and how strongly that gene is expressed.
We will come to those regulatory elements very shortly.
When the gene is "on", the protein it codes for is being actively produced.
When the gene is "off", the protein is not being made.
Much of biology is about flipping that switch and adjusting how hard it is held down.
Now, why does the cell bother with this RNA intermediate at all?
Why not translate directly from DNA?
There are several reasons, but two are most important.
First, in eukaryotic cells, DNA sits in the nucleus, while protein synthesis happens out in the cytoplasm — RNA acts as a portable messenger that can carry the instructions across that boundary.
Second, having an intermediate gives the cell another whole layer of control: it can modulate how much mRNA gets made, how long it lasts, where it goes, how efficiently it gets translated, and so on.
What is messenger RNA chemically?
It is almost the same molecule as DNA, with two small differences that matter enormously.
First, the sugar in RNA is ribose, not deoxyribose.
Ribose has a hydroxyl group at the 2-prime position of the sugar that DNA is missing — that extra hydroxyl is exactly what the "deoxy" prefix in DNA refers to.

That extra hydroxyl makes RNA more chemically reactive and less stable than DNA — RNA is much more prone to spontaneous hydrolysis — but it also lets RNA fold into more complex three-dimensional shapes than DNA usually does.
Second, RNA uses uracil — U — in place of thymine — T.
Uracil and thymine are almost identical; thymine just has an extra methyl group that uracil lacks.
Because U pairs with A in just the same way T does, the RNA alphabet works for base pairing without any modification to the rules: A pairs with U, G pairs with C.
Transcription, then, is the process of synthesising an RNA molecule whose sequence is dictated by complementary pairing against a DNA template strand.
The enzyme that does this is RNA polymerase.
We will come back to it in detail when we discuss transcriptional control.
Once an mRNA molecule has been made, it travels — in eukaryotes, out of the nucleus; in bacteria, just within the cell — to a ribosome.
The ribosome is one of the largest and most ancient macromolecular machines in biology — a complex composed of many proteins and several RNA molecules, joined into one functional unit.
It is here that translation happens — the conversion of the nucleotide sequence in the mRNA into an amino acid sequence that becomes a protein.
Notice the shift in alphabet: DNA and RNA use a four-letter nucleotide alphabet; proteins use a twenty-letter amino acid alphabet.
Most of the next two sections will be about how the cell translates between these two alphabets, and what the consequences are for designing biological systems.

sections.title: The Genetic Code
lines: Section six. The genetic code.
We just said that nucleic acids use four letters and proteins use twenty.
There is no way to get a one-to-one mapping out of that — four cannot encode twenty.
So the cell uses groups of letters.
Specifically, three nucleotides at a time.
A group of three nucleotides is called a codon.
Each codon specifies one amino acid.
With four possible bases at each of three positions, you have 4 cubed — that is, 64 possible codons.
Sixty-four possible codons, but only twenty amino acids — which means there is enormous redundancy in the genetic code.
Most amino acids are specified by more than one codon, sometimes by as many as six.
There are two interesting exceptions.
Tryptophan and methionine each have only one codon dedicated to them — UGG for tryptophan, AUG for methionine.

And those two amino acids are also among the less common ones in proteins, which is not a coincidence: a less frequently used amino acid does not benefit as much from having a redundant set of codons.
Methionine has a second, very special job beyond just being one of the twenty: in bacteria, every single protein starts with methionine.
The first amino acid added to every newly synthesised bacterial protein is methionine, encoded by an AUG codon.
Eukaryotes follow nearly the same convention.
This start convention matters because it tells the ribosome where to begin.

Recall that any stretch of DNA can be read in three different reading frames on each of its two strands — that is six possible frames total — depending on where you decide to start counting your three-nucleotide groups.
Without a defined start, the ribosome has no idea which frame is the real one.
The bacterial answer is twofold.

First, the mRNA carries a short sequence called the ribosome binding site, or RBS — sometimes also called the Shine-Dalgarno sequence after its discoverers — which lets the ribosome dock at the right place.
Second, just downstream of the RBS sits the first AUG codon.
When the ribosome lands at the RBS and finds that AUG, it sets the reading frame to start at that codon and translates from there.
Because methionine has only one codon, the start signal is unambiguous: there is no question about whether a given AUG "could" mean something else, because nothing else uses AUG.
That removes one source of ambiguity in an inherently noisy process.
The redundancy of the code is interesting in itself.
Because most amino acids have multiple codons, an organism can have a preference about which codons it uses most often, and different organisms tend to settle on different preferences.
This phenomenon is called codon usage bias, and it varies systematically between species.
E. coli, baker's yeast, humans, and a chloroplast all have somewhat different codon preferences.
This matters in synthetic biology in a very practical way.
If you take a gene from one organism and try to express it in another, the codons that were perfectly common in the original host may be very rare in the new host.
The new host has fewer of the tRNAs that read those rare codons, and the ribosome stalls when it hits one, and protein output drops.

The standard fix is called codon optimisation — you rewrite the DNA sequence of the gene, swapping rare codons in the new host for common ones, while preserving the amino acid sequence of the protein.
You will see codon optimisation come up repeatedly when we discuss expressing foreign genes in microbial chassis.
Now, the actual translation of codons to amino acids on the ribosome does not happen directly.
It happens through an intermediary class of small RNA molecules called transfer RNA — tRNA.
A tRNA molecule is short — about 76 nucleotides — and folds into a characteristic cloverleaf-like secondary structure with three loops.
One end of the tRNA, at its three-prime tail, gets chemically loaded with a specific amino acid.
Another part of the tRNA, at the bottom of one of the loops, exposes a sequence of three nucleotides called the anticodon.
The anticodon is — surprise — complementary to a codon in mRNA.

There are different tRNA species in the cell for each amino acid, and the matching between which tRNA gets loaded with which amino acid is enforced by another family of enzymes called aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases.

These synthetases are remarkably specific — they have to be, because if a tRNA was loaded with the wrong amino acid, the protein produced from that mRNA would have a mistake at every codon read by that tRNA, and the cell would gradually accumulate errors throughout its proteome.
So the genetic code, in practice, is not implemented in a lookup table.
It is implemented by the population of tRNAs and the synthetases that load them.
Whoever loads the tRNAs is, in a literal sense, defining what the code means.
There is one more subtlety to mention: wobble pairing.

At the third position of the codon — that is, the third nucleotide — the base pairing between codon and anticodon is sometimes a little loose, allowing one tRNA to recognise multiple synonymous codons.
That is part of why redundant codons can be efficiently translated by fewer than 61 different tRNAs.
We do not need to dwell on wobble here, but if you ever look up a codon table and wonder why some codons clearly behave as equivalents, wobble is the reason.

sections.title: Proteins
lines: Section seven. Proteins.
We have walked information from DNA, through RNA, to the door of the ribosome.
Now we step through the door and look at what comes out the other side: the protein.
Proteins do most of the actual work in a cell.
Whatever cellular activity you want to name — moving, eating, sensing, signalling, replicating, building — there is almost certainly a protein doing the work.
The diversity of protein function is genuinely staggering.
Some proteins are structural — they build physical material.
Spider silk is a protein.
Keratin, the structural protein in your hair and your fingernails, is a protein.
Collagen, holding tendons and skin together, is a protein.
Some proteins are motors — they convert chemical energy into mechanical force.
Myosin pulls on actin filaments to contract your muscles.
Kinesin walks along microtubule tracks, hauling cargo across the cell.
Some proteins are enzymes — catalysts that accelerate specific chemical reactions by many orders of magnitude.
Some proteins are regulators — they bind to other molecules and turn things on or off.

Hormones like insulin are regulatory proteins that act at long range, between tissues; transcription factors are regulatory proteins that act inside cells to turn genes on or off, which is exactly the kind of control synthetic biology spends its time engineering.
All of this functional diversity is generated from a strikingly limited toolkit: twenty different amino acids, in different orders.

Each amino acid has the same basic chemistry: a central carbon atom carrying an amine group on one side, a carboxylic acid group on the other, a hydrogen atom, and one distinguishing side chain that gives the amino acid its identity.

Some side chains are tiny and inert; some are hydrophobic and oily; some are positively charged; some are negatively charged; some can form hydrogen bonds; some carry sulphur atoms; some are aromatic rings.

The chemistry of joining amino acids into a chain is a condensation reaction: the amine of one amino acid attacks the carboxylic acid of the next, a peptide bond forms between them, and a single molecule of water is eliminated.
The ribosome catalyses this reaction at extraordinary speed, one peptide bond after another, with the order of amino acids dictated by the codons being read.
The result is a long, linear chain of amino acids called a polypeptide, or simply a protein.
Now here is where biology gets harder.
The function of a protein does not come directly from the sequence of amino acids — what biochemists call the primary structure.
Function comes from the three-dimensional folded shape that the chain adopts.

An amino acid chain spontaneously folds — driven by hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonds, electrostatics, and other forces — into a specific three-dimensional structure called its tertiary structure.
Along the way it forms recurrent local motifs — alpha helices, beta sheets — which are called the secondary structure.
Some proteins are made of multiple polypeptide chains assembled together, which is called the quaternary structure.

Folding is energetically favourable — the cell does not have to do anything special to make most proteins fold, though for some it employs chaperones to help — but the molecular details of how it happens are incredibly complicated.
And the inverse problem — predicting structure from sequence — has been one of the hardest problems in computational biology for fifty years.
Consider the combinatorics: an average-sized protein has about three hundred amino acids, each chosen from twenty possibilities, so the number of possible sequences is 20 to the 300th power.
That number is much, much larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe.
So the space of possible proteins is essentially infinite, and the cell has explored only a small, evolved corner of it.
That is the basis of biological diversity — and also the reason that engineering proteins for new functions remains hard.
Our progress in understanding protein structure has been driven mainly by X-ray crystallography, which can resolve protein structures at near-atomic resolution.
There are now well over 60,000 protein structures in the Protein Data Bank, the public repository of solved structures.
Nuclear magnetic resonance — NMR — and electron microscopy, including the cryo-electron microscopy revolution of recent years, have added many more, and at scales that crystallography struggles with.
Even with all of those structures in hand, rationally engineering a protein for a new function is still, as the textbook honestly puts it, largely a hit-and-miss affair.
We can change residues one at a time and observe what happens, we can graft loops, we can mutate active sites — but predicting from scratch what those changes will do is hard.

Computational structure prediction has improved enormously, exemplified by the long-running CASP competition — the Critical Assessment of Structure Prediction — and in very recent years by deep-learning-based methods that the original edition of this textbook did not yet have to discuss.

But for the purposes of synthetic biology design, you should treat protein engineering as a domain where you can sometimes make progress with structure-guided intuition, sometimes with directed evolution, and very often with a combination of the two, but rarely with a clean from-first-principles design.

When the book talks later about "parts" — promoters, ribosome binding sites, terminators, and so on — the protein-coding sequence sits in the middle of every part, and the protein that emerges from it is what the engineered system actually relies on doing real work.

sections.title: Transcriptional Control
lines: Section eight. Controlling the flow of information, starting with transcription.
An interesting observation from genome sequencing: complexity does not seem to live in raw gene count.
Humans have around 25,000 protein-coding genes.
E. coli, the well-studied bacterium, has about 4,800.
Five-fold difference for organisms that are obviously not five-fold different in sophistication.
So if complexity is not about how many genes you have, where does it live?
A large part of the answer is regulation.
Higher organisms devote a much larger fraction of their genomes to genes that control other genes — transcription factors, regulatory RNAs, chromatin modifiers, and so on.
The biological work of being a complex organism is largely the work of controlling when, where, and how strongly each gene is expressed.

For an engineer, this is encouraging: it suggests that to build interesting biological behaviour, you do not have to invent new gene products; you can often just build new control logic around existing ones.
Why do cells need that control?
In a eukaryote, gene regulation is how a skin cell ends up different from a brain cell despite having identical DNA — different sets of genes are being expressed in each.
In a bacterium, gene regulation is how the cell responds to its environment — different food sources, different stresses, different temperatures all drive different expression patterns.
And in synthetic biology, gene regulation is the thing you are designing.

Whether you want a strain that produces a chemical only when a sensor detects a signal, or a circuit that toggles between two states, or a programmable response to a stimulus — you are designing transcriptional and translational control.

Several research papers — Pfleger and colleagues in 2006, Pitera and colleagues in 2007 — established early that the fine balance of expression levels in a designed pathway is critical: too much enzyme can be as bad as too little, and tuning the levels is often the difference between a working strain and a failed one.
So we need to look at the actual mechanisms.
We will focus on prokaryotes — bacteria.
Eukaryotic transcription is far more elaborate, with nucleosomes, chromatin remodelling, splicing, and many other layers; the principles are similar but the machinery is heavier.
Most synthetic biology to date has been done in microbial chassis, so the prokaryotic story is the right place to start.
Transcription begins with the enzyme RNA polymerase, which I will sometimes shorten to RNAP.
It is the enzyme that reads a DNA template and produces a complementary mRNA strand.
Just like DNA polymerase, RNAP works in a 5-prime to 3-prime direction on the new RNA strand, which means it reads the template DNA strand 3-prime to 5-prime.
By convention, when biologists draw or sequence a gene, they show the "coding strand", which has the same sequence as the mRNA — with T's standing in for the U's that the actual mRNA would carry.
But it is the other strand, the "template strand", that the polymerase physically reads.
Now, a gene, as we said, is not just the coding sequence — it includes regulatory elements.
Of those, the most important is the promoter.
The promoter is the stretch of DNA, upstream of the coding sequence, where RNA polymerase docks and decides whether to start transcribing.
If you want to think of a gene as having an "on/off switch", the promoter is the switch.
In bacteria, a single promoter can drive a single gene, or it can drive an entire operon — a group of related genes clustered together and co-transcribed from the same promoter.
The classic example is the lac operon in E. coli, where the genes for lactose metabolism are organised in one operon, so the cell can turn on lactose digestion as a single coordinated decision.
Operons are essentially the bacterial idiom for grouping a set of functionally related genes under one regulatory decision.
Now, bacterial RNA polymerase has a quirk: by itself, it cannot find or bind a promoter.
It needs a helper protein called a sigma factor, written with the Greek letter sigma.
The sigma factor is what recognises the specific DNA sequences in the promoter region and guides RNA polymerase to bind there.
When RNA polymerase and a sigma factor are combined, the resulting active complex is called the holoenzyme.
E. coli has seven different sigma factors, each recognising a different family of promoters, and the cell uses them to coordinate large-scale gene regulation in response to different conditions.
Let me name them.
Sigma-70, also called RpoD, is the housekeeping sigma factor — it directs the polymerase to most genes that are needed under normal growth conditions.
Sigma-54, RpoN, handles nitrogen limitation.
Sigma-38, RpoS, handles stationary phase and starvation.
Sigma-32, RpoH, handles heat shock — it is the one whose level rises when the cell gets too hot.
Sigma-28, RpoF, handles flagellar gene expression.
Sigma-24, RpoE, handles extreme stress, particularly trouble at the cell envelope.
And sigma-19, FecI, handles ferric citrate transport — the iron uptake machinery.
By regulating the abundance and activity of each sigma factor, the cell can swap which whole programme of genes is being transcribed.
It is a tidy mechanism — turning on dozens of related genes via the abundance of one sigma factor — and you will see synthetic biologists co-opt it.
Where on the DNA does the sigma factor actually look?
The transcription start site — the first base that gets copied into mRNA — is designated the plus-one position.
Two key promoter elements sit upstream of plus-one.
There is a conserved sequence around the minus-ten position, and another around the minus-thirty-five position.
These are the recognition sites that sigma factors read.
The specific consensus sequence at minus-ten and minus-thirty-five differs slightly between sigma factors, which is what makes one sigma activate a different set of promoters than another.
And to make this even more layered, there are anti-sigma factors that bind to sigma factors and inhibit them, and anti-anti-sigma factors that bind to anti-sigma factors and restore activity.
It is regulatory turtles, several deep.
Sigma factors are not the only way to control transcription.
Two other classes of proteins are critical: activators and repressors.
An activator binds at or near a specific promoter and increases the rate of transcription from it.
A repressor binds at or near a specific promoter and decreases the rate of transcription from it.

Both activators and repressors are called transcription factors, and unlike the global, genome-wide effect of sigma factors, transcription factors typically act on a single gene or a small set of related genes.
Now one final point about how transcription factors give you a sharp on-off switch.
If a single repressor molecule binds a single site on DNA, the relationship between repressor concentration and gene expression is a hyperbolic curve — gradual, gentle, hard to fully shut off.
But many transcription factors bind cooperatively, often as dimers or tetramers, and to multiple binding sites in the operator region.
When the first repressor binds, it makes it easier for the second to bind too — protein-protein contacts plus protein-DNA contacts — and you end up with a sigmoidal, S-shaped response curve.
Sigmoidal means sharp.
A sigmoidal response curve gives you something close to a digital switch: below a threshold the gene is essentially on, above it the gene is essentially off, and the transition is fast.
This is why so many synthetic biology circuits depend on cooperatively binding repressors — the sharper the switch, the cleaner the circuit behaves.

Mark Ptashne's classic book on the bacteriophage lambda repressor, published in 1992, is the canonical reference if you want to dig deeper into how cooperativity gives biology digital-feeling switches; the textbook explicitly recommends it.
Modelling these interactions, even with simple Hill-type equations, will teach you a lot about how much engineering leverage you can get by tweaking binding affinities and cooperativity.

sections.title: Translational Control
lines: Section nine. Translational control.
Transcription gets you an mRNA molecule.
An mRNA molecule, sitting in the cytoplasm, is still just information — it has not yet become a functional protein.
That final step happens at the ribosome, and the process is called translation.
Translation is also a control point, and a very important one for synthetic biology.

In bacteria, the key element that controls translation is the ribosome binding site, or RBS, sometimes called the Shine-Dalgarno sequence — we mentioned it in passing earlier when discussing start codons.
The RBS is a short stretch of mRNA, sitting in the 5-prime untranslated region — the part of the mRNA that comes before the first AUG codon.
Its job is to be recognised by the ribosome, which uses it to dock onto the mRNA in the correct orientation and at roughly the right place for the first AUG to land in the ribosomal active site.

Once the ribosome is locked onto the RBS and positioned at the start codon, translation begins — codon by codon, the ribosome reads the mRNA, recruits the matching tRNA, and adds the corresponding amino acid to the growing polypeptide chain.
Here is the engineering-relevant point: how much protein you actually get out of a single mRNA depends critically on how strong the RBS is.
mRNAs are not long-lived molecules.
A typical bacterial mRNA has a half-life of just two to three minutes — much shorter than the protein that mRNA will produce.
So the number of protein molecules made from one mRNA molecule is finite — and the strength of the RBS, by setting how efficiently the ribosome engages, determines that number.
A strong RBS has a high affinity for the ribosome.
The ribosome binds it tightly, engages it quickly, and initiates many rounds of translation before the mRNA is degraded.
A weak RBS has a low affinity.
The ribosome binds it loosely, often dissociates without translating, and initiates fewer rounds before the mRNA is gone.
So a stronger RBS gives you more protein per mRNA, and a weaker RBS gives you less.
This makes the RBS one of the most powerful tuning knobs in synthetic biology.
If you want to dial down a gene's expression by, say, ten-fold, without changing the promoter or the protein, you can swap the RBS for a weaker variant.

Pfleger and colleagues, in a 2006 paper that became a touchstone in the field, showed that you can construct entire pathways with different RBS strengths controlling each enzyme, and by tuning them you can balance flux through a metabolic pathway, drastically improving the yield of the final product.
The RBS is one of the cleanest examples of a part you can swap to tune behaviour.
There is a consensus RBS sequence — the canonical Shine-Dalgarno sequence — and many natural and synthetic variants exist around it.
Small changes in sequence give you graded changes in strength.
If only RBS behaviour were perfectly predictable, life would be easy.
It is not, and the reason is one of the trickiest aspects of designing in biology: context dependence.
mRNA is single-stranded.
Single-stranded nucleic acid is free to fold back on itself — wherever two stretches of the same molecule are complementary, they can pair up and form a hairpin or other secondary structure.
If the sequence surrounding the RBS happens to be complementary to the RBS, the mRNA can fold up in a way that hides the RBS inside a secondary structure.
When that happens, the ribosome cannot bind, because it can only bind to the RBS when it is single-stranded and accessible.

So the same RBS, dropped in front of two different coding sequences, can give wildly different protein output — not because the RBS changed, but because the downstream sequence changed what secondary structure the mRNA folds into.
This is exactly the kind of problem that frustrates engineers: a part that behaves differently depending on what you connect it to.
The most influential attempt to put this on a quantitative footing is the RBS Calculator, published by Howard Salis, Ethan Mirsky, and Christopher Voigt in 2009.
Their idea was to model the thermodynamics of the process explicitly.
Compute the free energy of the mRNA in its folded state.
Compute the free energy of the mRNA bound to the ribosome.
Take the difference between those two free energies.
That difference is what controls the equilibrium between folded mRNA and ribosome-engaged mRNA, and it correlates — pretty well — with the actual translation initiation rate observed in cells.
The RBS Calculator can predict the relative strength of a synthetic RBS in a specific sequence context, and it gets predictions correct within roughly a factor of two or three of observed values.
A factor of two or three is enough to design with, and enough to do informed screening — but it is not enough for fully rational, hands-off design.
You will still build a small library of variants, test them, and pick the one that works.
But it is a real, useful advance over picking by trial and error.
The bigger lesson is this: in biology, parts are not always context-independent.
Designing reliably with biological parts often means measuring their behaviour in their actual deployment context, not just in isolation.

sections.title: RNA Regulation and Riboswitches
lines: Section ten. RNA as a regulator, and the riboswitches that synthetic biology has begun to put to work.
It is tempting to think of RNA as just the passive messenger — a tape that carries the message from DNA to ribosome and then gets destroyed.
For decades that is roughly how molecular biology thought about it.
We now know that view was incomplete, and in some ways quite wrong.
RNA does many things in the cell.
It does carry messages, yes.
But it also performs catalysis, and it actively regulates gene expression.
Its versatility is now believed to reflect a very ancient role.

There is a widely supported hypothesis, called the RNA world hypothesis, which proposes that very early in the history of life — perhaps before DNA existed at all — RNA served both as the storage molecule for genetic information and as the catalytic molecule that carried out cellular reactions.
In the RNA world, RNA was both database and machine.
DNA arose later as a more chemically stable carrier — better suited to long-term storage and to building genomes large enough for complex organisms.
Proteins arose to take over catalysis at scale, far more efficient and diverse than RNA at building specialised enzymes.
But RNA never quite handed everything over.
Some of the oldest and most central processes in modern cells are still done with RNA, or with RNA-protein hybrids.

The ribosome itself, which we have been talking about, is a perfect example: it is a massive ribonucleoprotein complex, but the catalytic centre that actually forms the peptide bond is made of RNA, not protein.
The ribosome is, at its heart, a ribozyme — an RNA enzyme.
Transfer RNA, similarly, is RNA doing a sophisticated structural and recognition job that proteins do not.
Even outside the ribosome, RNA does catalysis: some RNAs splice themselves; some cut other RNAs; we call these catalytic RNAs ribozymes generally.
But the discovery that has shaped molecular biology most over the last twenty-five years is RNA's role in regulating gene expression through small RNAs.
In eukaryotes, the discovery of small interfering RNA — siRNA — and the related microRNA system has opened up an entirely new layer of gene control.
These short RNAs bind to specific mRNAs by base pairing, recruit protein machinery, and either degrade the mRNA or block its translation.
Beyond the basic biology, siRNA has become a workhorse tool: in the lab, you can design a short RNA to silence essentially any gene you want, with high specificity.

Prokaryotes do not have the same elaborate machinery as eukaryotes do, but they also use small RNAs to regulate gene expression, and synthetic biologists have built versions of small-RNA control in bacteria.
An influential paper by Travis Bayer and Christina Smolke in 2005 showed that you can rationally engineer small RNAs that bind to specific mRNAs and modulate translation in predictable ways.
An attractive feature of RNA-based regulation is its speed.
RNA molecules are made quickly and degraded quickly — their typical half-lives are short.

That means RNA-based regulation can turn on and off much faster than protein-based regulation, where you have to first transcribe the regulator, then translate it, then have the regulator find its target.
Proteins are also costly: they take more energy to build, last longer than you might want, and clutter up the cell with leftover regulator after the moment of decision has passed.
RNA regulators sidestep some of that cost.
And then there is a particular class of RNA regulators that is especially elegant: riboswitches.

A riboswitch is a stretch of RNA, usually sitting in the 5-prime untranslated region of an mRNA, that can fold into two different shapes — and that physically changes shape when a small metabolite molecule binds to it.
Imagine a length of RNA sitting just upstream of an RBS.
When no small molecule is present, the RNA adopts one structure that leaves the RBS exposed, ribosome happy, translation proceeds.

When the relevant small molecule is around — say, an amino acid that the cell happens to be metabolising — the RNA binds the small molecule and refolds into a different structure that buries the RBS in a hairpin, ribosome cannot dock, translation stops.
The riboswitch has, in effect, sensed the concentration of a metabolite directly, without needing a protein sensor at all.
In nature, riboswitches are often used as feedback regulators on metabolic pathways: when the metabolic product accumulates, the riboswitch shuts off translation of the enzymes that produce it.
It is a beautiful, low-cost feedback control built entirely out of RNA and a small molecule.
Tucker and Breaker described many natural riboswitches in a 2005 review that the textbook recommends.
And synthetic biologists have engineered riboswitches into circuits — Callura and colleagues in 2010 showed riboswitches incorporated into programmable bacterial control modules.
If you treat them as engineered parts, riboswitches give you a way to make protein expression directly conditional on chemical signals, with no extra protein machinery required.
When you put all of this together — the central dogma, the levels of control, the role of RNA — what you should see is a layered architecture.
Information flows from DNA to RNA to protein.
But at every level, the cell has knobs.
It can tune transcription via promoters, sigma factors, activators, and repressors.
It can tune translation via the RBS and via secondary structure in mRNA.
It can tune everything again with regulatory RNAs and riboswitches.
Every one of those knobs is, in principle, available to a synthetic biologist as a design surface.
The rest of this book is, in a real sense, a tour of how to design at those surfaces.
That is the end of chapter one.
